summary: The ACSC Information Security Manual (ISM) has been updated from the December 2025 release to the March 2026 release. This update includes new versions of the main ISM document, its corresponding changes document, and associated templates (System Security Plan Annex, Cloud Controls Matrix), all showing slight increases in file size. Additionally, the descriptive text outlining the ISM's protection scope has been subtly refined by removing the specific mention of 'applications and data' when referring to protecting information technology and operational technology systems.
analysis:
The update of the ACSC ISM to the March 2026 version indicates a regular and ongoing evolution of Australia's authoritative cyber security framework. The new release cycle, moving from December 2025 to March 2026, implies that the ASD continues to refine its guidance in response to the changing threat landscape and best practices. 

The consistent increase in file sizes across the main ISM PDF (2.3MB to 2.32MB), the ISM Changes PDF (741KB to 770KB), the System Security Plan annex template (159KB to 163KB), and the Cloud Controls Matrix template (203KB to 206KB) strongly suggests that substantive content modifications, additions, or clarifications have been made within these documents. Organizations should consult the 'ISM March 2026 changes' document for a detailed breakdown of these revisions.

The removal of the phrase ', applications and data' from the descriptions of what the ISM aims to protect (specifically from 'information technology and operational technology systems, applications and data' to 'information technology and operational technology systems') is a notable linguistic change. While applications and data security are fundamental aspects of any information security manual, this change in the summary descriptions might represent a simplification of language or a strategic refinement in how the ISM's core scope is communicated at a high level. It's plausible that 'applications and data' are now considered implicitly covered when securing 'systems', or the focus of the descriptive text has shifted to the systems as the primary entities to be protected against threats, which in turn protects the applications and data residing on them. Users of the ISM should review the full document for any changes in the depth or approach to securing applications and data.
